topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The string quartet swelled, a saccharine sound that grated on my nerves. I clutched my lukewarm champagne flute, the bubbles doing nothing to lift the heavy weight in my chest. Every forced smile I managed felt like cracking plaster. Then, across the crowded ballroom, I saw him. Liam. He was laughing, his arm around the bride, looking effortlessly radiant. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake of air that left my throat tight. He turned, his eyes sweeping the room, and for one agonizing second, they landed on mine. The laughter seemed to drain out of him, replaced by a flicker of something unreadable—a momentary pause before he offered a small, polite nod. I immediately looked down at my shoes, suddenly intensely aware of the cheap fabric of my dress and the tremor in my hands. It was a performance I hadn't rehearsed, a collision of past and present that felt entirely too loud for this elegant evening.

[story 2]

I navigated the periphery of the reception, seeking refuge in the dim shadows near the terrace doors. The music was too loud, the forced gaiety suffocating. I watched the happy couple dance, the way they moved with such easy, undeniable connection, and a dull ache settled deep beneath my ribs. Then, there he was. Liam. He was talking animatedly to a group of old university friends, his posture open and confident. When his gaze swept past me, it snagged, and time seemed to slow. My carefully constructed composure fractured. I felt a strange, cold numbness spread through my limbs, making it hard to move. I retreated further into the cool night air, wishing the floor would simply open up and swallow me whole. I focused intently on the pattern of the paving stones beneath my feet, counting the cracks, anything to avoid the inevitable, awkward proximity.

[story 3]

The noise level was relentless, a tidal wave of forced merriment. I kept my head down, focusing on the intricate floral arrangements lining the reception hall, trying to anchor myself to something tangible. It was exhausting, this constant effort to appear buoyant when every muscle in my body felt weighted down by exhaustion. Then, I saw him standing near the buffet, looking slightly out of place amidst the celebratory chaos. Liam. He caught my eye from across the room, and the familiar landscape of regret washed over me, thick and unwelcome. My jaw tightened involuntarily. I wanted to melt into the marble floor, to simply cease existing for a moment. I took a deliberate, slow sip of water, forcing my expression into a neutral mask, praying that the sheer effort of maintaining composure would be enough to keep the connection between us firmly severed.
